ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai green curry chicken with rice. FOODON_00002809 (chicken curry dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken curry) is used as the closest parent; no specific Thai green curry with rice code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Thai green curry chicken with rice as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai green curry chicken with rice is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed jasmine rice and 250 g of green curry chicken with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Green Curry with Chicken and Rice — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries: Chicken, broiler, meat only, cooked (FDC ID 171477); Coconut milk, canned (FDC ID 170172); White rice, cooked, long-grain (FDC ID 168878); and Thai green curry paste reference values from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai green curry chicken with rice as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked chicken breast, canned coconut milk, cooked jasmine rice, and green curry paste, weighted by typical recipe proportions (approximately 35% cooked chicken, 30% curry sauce with coconut milk, 35% cooked rice). The resulting per-100g values reflect a blended composite. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and curry paste. Saturated fat is elevated due to coconut milk content.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (jasmine, cooked) GI ~68; composite dish GI estimated at 58 accounting for fat and protein attenuation from coconut milk and chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai green curry chicken with rice as a composite dish. Jasmine rice has a published GI of approximately 68 (University of Sydney GI Database). The addition of coconut milk fat (~5.8 g fat per 100g) and protein (~8.5 g per 100g) from chicken attenuates the glycemic response. A composite GI of 58 is estimated using the mixed-meal attenuation principle documented in Atkinson et al. (2021) and Jenkins et al. peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 68.4) / 100 ≈ 39.7, rounded to 39.8 for a 450 g serving providing approximately 68.4 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and green curry paste
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1395 mg per standard 450 g serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced fish sauce are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from jasmine rice, partially attenuated by coconut milk fat and chicken protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 39.8 per standard serving is moderate to high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to half and increasing the curry-to-rice ratio to lower the overall glycemic impact of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk (approximately 4.1 g saturated fat per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content from coconut milk (~18.5 g per standard serving) exceeds the recommended daily limit of 20 g for cardiovascular disease management; individuals with CVD should limit portion size or choose a reduced-coconut-milk preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai green curry chicken with rice (แกงเขียวหวานไก่ข้าว, Kaeng Khiao Wan Kai Khao) is a classic Thai dish consisting of tender chicken pieces simmered in a fragrant, creamy green curry sauce made from coconut milk, green curry paste, kaffir lime leaves, Thai basil, lemongrass, galangal, and fish sauce, served alongside steamed jasmine rice. The curry sauce is characteristically bright green, mildly spicy to moderately hot, rich and creamy from the coconut milk, with a complex herbal and citrus aroma. The dish is a staple of Thai cuisine and is widely available in Thai restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g restaurant serving provides approximately 639 kcal, 38 g protein, 26 g fat (of which approximately 18.5 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 68 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 1395 mg per serving due to fish sauce and curry paste, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load is moderate to high due to the jasmine rice component, though partially attenuated by the fat and protein content of the curry. Clinically, this dish is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its glycemic load, high concern for hypertension due to sodium, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from coconut milk. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free but contains fish and crustacean allergens from fish sauce and shrimp paste in the curry paste.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai green curry chicken with rice as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked chicken, canned coconut milk, cooked jasmine rice, and green curry paste, weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with mixed-meal attenuation applied. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source record for this exact dish was available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard recipe ingredients. 4. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate; the closest direct approved-source GI is for jasmine rice (GI ~68, University of Sydney GI Database); the composite dish GI is attenuated by fat and protein per published mixed-meal methodology. 5. Halal status is marked false as shrimp paste in commercial green curry paste may not be halal-certified; individual restaurant verification is required. 6. The food_name has been normalised from the input menu name 'R8 Green Curry Chicken w Rice' to the canonical English form 'Green Curry Chicken with Rice'. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are approximate.
